item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I third the notion that the source hidden text is unnecesary and quite blunt in telling you exactly what's going on, given the fact that anyone that has read at least 20 skips knows that there are only 13 O5's and would immediately notice the discrepancy. That's why it's so powerful!

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: This is very true, I'm going to tread more carefully in future proposals of this nature.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
the tone of those tales differs from the tone of the article, while still enforcing the anomalies effects.

Although you have amended it, I do I owe you my reasoning for my objection.

Differences in tone is nice and all, but the crosslinks should have make sense in the articles' context. It felt as though you just went for similar anomalies without considering the SCP or characters behind those stories.

And crosslinking without making strong sense risk weakening all articles involved.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: I've revised the proposed crosslink based on ARD's suggestion, and reset the timer!

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: It seems to me that SCP-1500 is a much more logical crosslink.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: This is fair, the tone of those tales differs from the tone of the article, while still enforcing the anomalies effects. if there are more relevant/less tone breaking crosslinks I would be happy to consider them, and extend the timer accordingly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit late to the conversation. But yeah, I miss the old nickname. I almost thought the article was somewhat deleted.

But alas, the author's got to do what he has to do.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: Speaking as a regular reader, is there an underlying logic/rationale behind crosslinking this with the Cicada SCP sub-lore? Without a justification, this feels very arbitrary and too radical of a shift in meaning that might undermine the articles involved.

item.title: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

The following edits will crosslink this article to two tales involving SCP-2359.

Editing this:

SCP-2359’s primary anomalous property is a cognitohazard that propagates to anyone that makes visual, auditory or informational contact with it.

To this:

SCP-2359’s primary anomalous property is a cognitohazard that propagates to anyone that makes visual, auditory or informational contact with it.1

Footnotes
1. Similar to SCP-1500.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So with that in mind, what's your honest opinion? Do you still think the old version was better?

Personally, my reasons for changing it to the current title was that the previous one was overlong to the point that I worried it was too clunky, and I also feared that it was a bit too on the nose about the twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh I did read that poem, it was in the dark, dusty corner of my memories that I couldn't relate the title to it.
Thanks for the explanation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a reference to a poem.

Yesterday, upon the stair
I met a man who wasn't there
He wasn't there again today
I wish, I wish he'd go away

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know and cannot guess why you changed the title into "Yesterday, Upon the Stair", and until I find the reason I insist the older title ("Latest Staff Notice: Do Not Observe") was better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't change it to "every 1.21 Megaseconds" because while metric, it is just silly. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did consider making all information of SCP-2359 hazardous, but the removal of the indistinct line between safe and hazardous knowledge - and the possibility that the foundation is wrong in what it believes to be safe - made my own article seem bland to me. Also, it would remove impact of the twist, which implies that the article has been doctored by SCP-2359 to make it hazardous. If it was always hazardous, that twist would be rendered ineffectual.

My only regret in changing from "memetic" to "cognitohazardous" is that the later is a veritable eyesore on the page. At the very least, it doesn't flow anywhere near as well. That's a trifling issue, though, so it doesn't merit concern.

Anyway, the changes have been made now, along with some formatting changes to place the two interviews into collapsibles. Nice to have the article up to snuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The alternative is to have every description of SCP-2359 be infectious. That way it's a perfectly acceptable memetic hazard.

I was almost the dumbass that thought they knew better than the highest authority.

By all means put forth your arguments. I'm not the highest authority on memetic threats by any means, if there even is such a thing. I am always up for being schooled. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I didn't expect the very first person to post feedback to notice the tidbit in the source. Props for that!

Anyway, I'm doing some revisions right now. I'll remove the quotations from the first interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I'm quite proud of the concept, although the delivery would have fallen flat without generous help from the Site19 chat.
I'm about to do a major revision based on what others have said in the discussion. As part of that, addendums A and B will enclosed in collapsibles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy providing the source reveal as a little tidbit for anyone that happens to snoop it out - which is a lot more people than I anticipated.

I'm about to do a set of revisions based on what people have mentioned in the discussion, and one of those will be dialing the source twist to something a touch less melodramatic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose that comes down to your headcanon for the O5s. If they're just regular humans in a position of power, there's every chance they could be infected before noticing the spreading discrepancy. If they're anomalous in some way that would hinder SCP-2359's effects, then perhaps my twist has been neutered. All up to your interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me, I find that curious. I'm from New Zealand - a purely metric country - and we use fortnightly all the time. I've never even thought of it as imperial, considering it's just a shorthand word for describing "2 weeks".

Do you think it needs to be changed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was doing some research so that I could present the argument that it's debatable whether SCP-2359 possesses an inherent cognitohazard, or full blown memetic anomaly - and then I noticed that you're listed as the contact for suggesting new tags. I was almost the dumbass that thought they knew better than the highest authority.

It's a quick fix to replace all instances of 'memetic infection/impression/etc' with 'cognitohazard', so I'll do that shortly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're quite right. Buxton was a name I created a few years ago for a fictional Tennessee county/town, alongside "Delford" And "Cainesfield".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no such place as Buxton County

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You may have your victory for exactly 1.21 Ms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean you don't remember what happened the last time we tried that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: let me have my tiny victories ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if we put it in SCP-055's containment chamber?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Metric time basically never caught on, it certainly isn't required by SI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maaan, I hate when I get all fired up to point out a flaw in an article, and then that flaw turns out to be the whole point of it. XD The format screw works! All I can gripe about is the use of "fortnightly", because that sure as hell ain't metric.

I just love that this guy has massive inherent mind-control powers and the best he could do was mayor of a small town in Tennessee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the other O5's eventually notice that some people think that there's 14 of them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is how you do an intelligent infohazard. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent ear for natural-sounding dialogue and inflection, and an engrossing narrative to boot–this gets an enthusiastic +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entity’s memetic infection also cannot propagate through second-hand documentation, description or repetition of what subjects believe SCP-2359 has said.

Then it's not memetic, just a cognitohazard. A core part of memetic threats is that they have a vector of infection that goes beyond the anomaly itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit skeptical through this, until

O5-14

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this, it's a bit blunt, but I like what you did with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really glad this came out so well. You put a lot of effort into this, and I enjoyed seeing it evolve in the chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty great, especially for a first SCP. (And for someone whose had an account for only 2 weeks as well)1+1

Although I think the interviews could be collapsed, for the sake of the mobile users, which also happens to be me at the moment.

Footnotes
1. I'm not saying all first SCPs are usually doomed to fail, but often they aren't this well written

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Figured it out the moment I saw O5-14 being mentioned, felt the super secret page source reveal to be a little on-the-nose. Good job, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once I read through the description, my initial thought was "this is a blatant copy of SCP-1500." But then I continued reading and I found that this was executed pretty darn well. There are a couple of errors though, which I will point out here:

I don't see the need for the quotation marks in the first interview log.

As far as I can tell, there have only been thirteen members of the O5 Council (except for the highly secretive O5-14 mentioned in Dr. Clef's Proposal, but I don't think he/she would be concerned with this particular SCP due to his/her secretive location and this SCP's harmful effects).

EDIT: Yeah, forgot to read the page source. I knew there was something oddly suspicious about that area of white space.

However these are all nitpicky and you don't have to change them; just thought I would provide some suggestions.

Overall though, there was enough here to justify my upvote.
